content.text: Agents store conversation history, tool registries, retrieved documents, and execution traces in Python collections. Choosing the right structure affects ordering guarantees, lookup speed, deduplication, and whether state can be accidentally mutated across pipeline steps. Mastering these four built-in types is the single highest-leverage Python skill for writing reliable agentic code.
Python provides four general-purpose collection types as language built-ins. Each has a distinct memory model, lookup semantics, and mutability contract:
— ordered, mutable sequence backed by a dynamic array. Supports O(1) index access, O(1) amortized append, and O(n) arbitrary insertion or membership test.
— ordered, immutable sequence. Identical memory layout to list but fixed after creation. Hashable when all elements are hashable, so usable as dict keys and set members.
— unordered collection of unique hashable objects backed by a hash table. O(1) average membership test and add; no indexing or ordering.
— mapping from hashable keys to arbitrary values. O(1) average lookup, insert, and delete. Insertion-ordered since Python 3.7 (CPython detail promoted to language spec in 3.8).
in the standard library, all of which extend one of the four core types.

Agent code is almost entirely data movement: messages flow through lists, tool schemas live in dicts, capability allowlists are sets, and route pairs are tuples. The wrong container choice creates subtle bugs that only appear at scale:
as a list instead of a set costs O(n) per turn. With 50 tools and 1000 agent steps that is 50 000 unnecessary comparisons.
Passing a list of messages by reference to a sub-agent that appends to it will corrupt the parent history -- the mutation is invisible at the call site.
Using a list as a dict key --
Trusting set iteration order produces non-deterministic prompt content, leading to unreproducible evaluation results.
preserve the chronological order of conversation turns, plan steps, and retrieved chunk rankings.
provide O(1) key-based access, making tool result lookup by call ID, config access, and JSON round-trips fast and natural.
enforce uniqueness automatically and expose algebraic operators (union
) invaluable for permission diffing and URL frontier management.
are lightweight, immutable, and hashable, making them safe to share across agent boundaries without defensive copying.
All four types are iterable, work with comprehensions, and accept type-hint parameters in Python 3.9+ (e.g.
) giving mypy and pyright full static analysis coverage.
), O(n) arbitrary insertion, and unbounded memory growth if never trimmed.
require hashable keys -- lists and dicts cannot be keys. Each entry carries overhead of roughly 50-200 bytes depending on key and value sizes.
are unordered and unindexable. Converting to a sorted list costs O(n log n). They cannot hold mutable elements.
cannot be extended after creation. To "append" you must construct a new tuple; use list during construction, convert to tuple for the final stored value.
-- lists and dicts passed between functions share the same underlying storage. Mutations in one scope are visible in all aliases.
is a C array of object pointers. When capacity is exceeded, CPython allocates a larger block (approximately 1.125x growth factor) and moves all pointers. This is why
is O(1) amortized but occasionally O(n). Slicing (
) always creates a new list with copies of the referenced pointers -- it does not share storage.

# List fundamentals for agent message history
history: list[dict[str, str]] = []

# append -- amortized O(1)
history.append({"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful coding agent."})
history.append({"role": "user",   "content": "What is a decorator?"})
history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": "A decorator wraps a function..."})

# Negative indexing -- O(1)
last_turn = history[-1]              # most recent message
prev_turn = history[-2]              # second-most-recent

# Slicing -- creates new list, O(k) where k = slice length
tail = history[-10:]                 # last 10 turns for context window

# extend vs append
more_turns = [{"role": "user", "content": "Show an example."}]
history.extend(more_turns)           # adds items individually
# history.append(more_turns)        # WRONG: adds the list as a single element

# pop -- O(1) from end; O(n) from arbitrary index
history.pop()                        # remove last
# history.pop(0)                    # remove first -- avoid in hot loops; use deque

# insert -- O(n) because all subsequent pointers shift
history.insert(0, {"role": "system", "content": "New system prompt"})

# Checking membership -- O(n); convert to set when doing this repeatedly
has_system = any(m["role"] == "system" for m in history)

print(f"History has {len(history)} turns")
uses open addressing with compact storage introduced in CPython 3.6. Average-case insert, lookup, and delete are O(1). Since 3.7, iteration over
is a hash table of elements with no associated value. It shares the same O(1) average-case complexity as dict keys. Sets expose algebraic operators directly:
is the immutable variant -- hashable, usable as dict key or set element.

Tuples use the same pointer array as lists but with no capacity padding and no mutation methods. Because CPython interns small tuples of immutable objects, creating the same small tuple repeatedly can reuse an existing object. Unpacking (
) is O(n) but optimised in bytecode with the

# Tuple fundamentals -- immutable records and multi-value returns

# Construction
route = ("planner", "executor", "validator")  # pipeline stage names
lat_lon = (40.7128, -74.0060)                 # fixed coordinate pair
singleton = (42,)                             # trailing comma required for 1-tuple

# Index access -- O(1)
first_stage = route[0]        # "planner"
last_stage  = route[-1]       # "validator"

# Unpacking -- O(n), bytecode-optimised
model, fallback = ("claude-opus-4-5", "claude-haiku-4-5")

# Extended unpacking (Python 3)
head, *rest = route            # head = "planner", rest = ["executor", "validator"]

# Tuples as dict keys -- only works because tuples are hashable
cache: dict[tuple[str, int], str] = {}
cache[("search", 3)] = "cached result"

# Named tuples for readable records
from collections import namedtuple
ToolCall = namedtuple("ToolCall", ["name", "call_id", "args"])
tc = ToolCall(name="search", call_id="call_001", args={"q": "python"})
print(tc.name, tc.call_id)   # attribute access, not just index

# Return multiple values from a function -- Python returns a tuple
def get_model_and_tokens(response: dict) -> tuple[str, int]:
    return response["model"], response["usage"]["total_tokens"]

model_name, tokens_used = get_model_and_tokens(
    {"model": "claude-opus-4-5", "usage": {"total_tokens": 300}}
)
The diagram below summarises the four collection types, their internal contracts, and primary use cases in agentic systems.
Order must be preserved; append-only growth is natural
O(1) lookup by name at every step
Ranked order preserved; indexable for context truncation
Capability token that must not change
Immutable and hashable; tamper-evident permission state
A complete agent state container showing idiomatic use of all four types:
Nested trace structure and ordered deduplication

# Nested list-of-dicts -- common agent execution trace format
from typing import Any

trace: list[dict[str, Any]] = [
    {
        "step": 1,
        "tool": "search",
        "args": {"query": "python asyncio"},
        "result": {"hits": 12, "top_url": "https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html"},
        "latency_ms": 230,
    },
    {
        "step": 2,
        "tool": "summarise",
        "args": {"text": "..."},
        "result": {"summary": "asyncio provides ..."},
        "latency_ms": 890,
    },
]

# Aggregate stats from trace
total_ms = sum(s["latency_ms"] for s in trace)
tools_used = {s["tool"] for s in trace}   # set -- deduped
print(f"Total: {total_ms}ms, tools: {tools_used}")

# Ordered dedup -- critical for RAG chunk pipelines
def unique_ordered(ids: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Return ids with duplicates removed, preserving first-occurrence order."""
    seen: set[str] = set()
    result: list[str] = []
    for item in ids:
        if item not in seen:   # O(1)
            seen.add(item)
            result.append(item)
    return result

# dict.fromkeys preserves insertion order (Python 3.7+) -- one-liner alternative
def unique_ordered_v2(ids: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    return list(dict.fromkeys(ids))

raw = ["doc_3", "doc_1", "doc_3", "doc_2", "doc_1"]
print(unique_ordered(raw))    # ["doc_3", "doc_1", "doc_2"]
print(unique_ordered_v2(raw)) # same result
A multi-step retrieval agent that fetches from multiple sources, deduplicates chunk IDs by score, filters disallowed sources, and truncates to a context window:
Membership check on list in a loop
inside a loop that runs thousands of times is O(n^2). Convert to a set before the loop.
copies only the top-level dict. Inner lists and dicts are still shared. Use
when passing state to a sub-agent.
Using a list as a dict key
produces an arbitrary order. If determinism matters, use
creates one list shared across all calls. Always write
. In agent code where configs are partial, always use
Forgetting that dict values are references
Appending a list instead of extending
adds the entire list as one element. Use
. Full annotations catch structural mistakes before runtime.
-- designate one module or class as the owner of each mutable collection. Other code should receive copies for lists and dicts, or frozensets for sets.
Prefer tuple returns for small fixed-size results
is idiomatic and enables direct unpacking at the call site.
if k not in d: d[k] = []; d[k].append(v)
rather than the full history to sub-agents. Unbounded lists consume memory and context tokens.
Build with list, store as tuple
-- collect items in a list during construction, convert to tuple when the shape is fixed:

generic aliases (superseded by built-in syntax in 3.9+).
-- validates dict-shaped data against typed models at runtime; widely used for agent tool schemas and API request/response validation.
-- static type checkers that enforce collection type annotations, catching incorrect container choices before runtime.
are not JSON serialisable by default.
), better IDE support, and static type checking. Use dataclasses for stable schemas; use dicts for dynamic or external data.

is O(1) per pop vs O(n) for

adds field names without giving up hashability or memory efficiency. Prefer
(sortedcontainers package) -- when you need a sorted list that maintains order on insert. Rare in agent code but useful for priority-based task queues.
Validate dict keys from external input
-- agent tool arguments arrive as JSON dicts. Always validate key existence and type before accessing:
. Missing or wrongly typed keys from LLM output will raise
-- an adversarial agent loop or runaway recursive plan can append to a list indefinitely until memory is exhausted. Enforce maximums:
Never use eval to build dicts from strings
. eval executes arbitrary code, and LLM output cannot be trusted as code.
-- always deep-copy the state dict or message list before passing it to a sub-agent. Sharing mutable state means a sub-agent bug corrupts the parent agent silently.
Use frozenset for stable capability tokens
of allowed tool names cannot be accidentally mutated after construction, making permission state tamper-evident within a run.
Think of the four types as four physical metaphors:
is a numbered ticket queue -- items have position, you append at the back and peek by number.
is a labelled filing cabinet -- you retrieve by name instantly, and each drawer holds one value.
is a bag of unique tokens -- dropping a duplicate token in changes nothing; you can only ask "is this token present?"
is a sealed envelope -- contents are fixed the moment you close it; you can read but not modify.

Decision rule: if order matters -- list or tuple. If you need to look up by name -- dict. If you need to know whether something is present without caring about position -- set. If the structure should not change after creation -- tuple or frozenset.
Messages and ordered steps belong in lists; labelled fields and tool results belong in dicts; uniqueness constraints and permission checks belong in sets; fixed-shape records belong in tuples.
Dict and set membership is O(1) average; list membership is O(n). Convert to set before hot-loop membership checks.
Lists and dicts are mutable and share references on assignment. Use
Tuples are hashable (when their elements are) and can serve as dict keys or frozenset members -- use this for stable cache keys.
) make collection choices explicit and enable static analysis with mypy/pyright.
Validate dict keys from LLM output before accessing; bound list sizes to prevent memory exhaustion; never eval JSON from untrusted sources.
